response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've been using the internet for many years, but lately I find myself spending just an hour each day looking at these online platforms. Social media has become a bit overwhelming for me, especially with all this fake news floating around. It seems like whenever I open my account, there's something new to worry about—whether it's from politicians or even everyday people who don't know what they're talking about.

I remember when the internet first came into our home in Philadelphia back in the day; we were curious but not sure how much to trust everything online. Now with social media everywhere, it feels like there are so many conflicting stories that could change your mind or even make you depressed if you don't watch out carefully.

From my perspective as an 85-year-old man who's been through a lot and still reads the papers occasionally (though I wish they were clearer sometimes), one thing is certain: it helps to be skeptical but not cynical. Since I have limited time on social media now due to some depression issues, focusing only on sources that I know are reliable makes more sense.

I think young people today need guidance about this just like my parents taught me things when we were learning about radio and television days ago. Maybe having a list of trusted sites or even using fact-checking tools could help cut through the noise.",
  "reasoning_summary": "First, I considered how an elderly person with limited media exposure might view fake content differently than younger users.

Then I thought about managing time due to depression and primary school education level affecting comprehension depth.

Next, I drew from my native-born American background where media literacy was traditionally not as emphasized but now is crucial given digital age complexities.

Also incorporated the moderate conscientiousness trait which values organization in addressing information overload issues.",
  "tone": "Calm, reflective with a hint of concern",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "psychological_feature", "education_level", "time_onsocialmedia"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been using the internet for many years, but lately I find myself spending just an hour each day looking at these online platforms. Social media has become a bit overwhelming for me, especially with all this fake news floating around. It seems like whenever I open my account, there's something new to worry about—whether it's from politicians or even everyday people who don't know what they're talking about.

I remember when the internet first came into our home in Philadelphia back in the day; we were curious but not sure how much to trust everything online. Now with social media everywhere, it feels like there are so many conflicting stories that could change your mind or even make you depressed if you don't watch out carefully.

From my perspective as an 85-year-old man who's been through a lot and still reads the papers occasionally (though I wish they were clearer sometimes), one thing is certain: it helps to be skeptical but not cynical. Since I have limited time on social media now due to some depression issues, focusing only on sources that I know are reliable makes more sense.

I think young people today need guidance about this just like my parents taught me things when we were learning about radio and television days ago. Maybe having a list of trusted sites or even using fact-checking tools could help cut through the noise.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
First, I considered how an elderly person with limited media exposure might view fake content differently than younger users.

Then I thought about managing time due to depression and primary school education level affecting comprehension depth.

Next, I drew from my native-born American background where media literacy was traditionally not as emphasized but now is crucial given digital age complexities.

Also incorporated the moderate conscientiousness trait which values organization in addressing information overload issues.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, reflective with a hint of concern
